goals.title: Pattern Recognition & Self-Awareness
goals.description: Learn to identify patterns where I'm derailing myself and become more self-aware

history.note: Patterns showed up months or years later. Horrifying unconscious realisations.
Hours or days now, not months. Not concerned about this in the slightest.

goals.title: Emotional Processing
goals.description: Learn to process emotions without suppressing — no more implosions, sickness, shingles

history.note: Suppressed everything. Body kept the score.
Deep dives activate dormant emotions. Anger discovery was surprisingly accessible. Proud of leaning in with no idea what I'm doing.

goals.title: Sharing Myself With People I Love
goals.description: Learn to open up and share what I'm going through with the people around me

history.note: Kept everyone at arm's length. All Spartans, no Pat.
Boys have never been closer. Want more depth with Dad and Alex — feelings, not achievements.

goals.title: Meaningful Goal-Setting
goals.description: Create goals I actually care about — not all-or-nothing achievement machines

history.note: Goals were external validation shopping lists. 95% of energy on things that didn't mean anything to me.
Genuine ambition appeared on its own. Mapped a world trip in 10 minutes. Old Pat wouldn't have dared dream it.

milestones.title: Sold Spartans, Found Pat
milestones.summary: Sold the gym. Lost the identity shield. For the first time in years, conversations couldn't default to business.
milestones.story:
Spartans was everything. My identity, my social currency, my deflection tool. When someone asked 'how are you?' I could always steer it to the gym, the members, the numbers. It was the world's most socially acceptable way to never talk about myself.

Selling to Zac on Jan 5 removed all of that overnight. Suddenly I was just... Pat. No business card, no role, no safe topic to hide behind. I was terrified my boys might not accept me without it — that I was only as valuable as what I could offer. That fear tells you everything about where I was at.

Turns out removing Spartans was like removing training wheels. I didn't have a bottomless topic of unreliable external shit to deflect with anymore, and I finally got the opportunity to let my boys in and share about myself. What I found on the other side was the opposite of what I feared.
milestones.quote: So much of my own paper identity was very much all about Spartans, and very little about Pat.

milestones.title: Session 1 — The Starting Line
milestones.summary: First session with Meg. Set 5 goals. Had no idea what I was doing but trusted the instinct to show up.
milestones.story:
I'd been meaning to see someone for a while but kept finding reasons not to. Big life transitions — sold the gym, full-time investor now, planning a move to Thailand with Laura. On paper everything was going great. Inside, something wasn't right and I couldn't name it.

Meg asked me what I wanted to work on. I set 5 goals that felt honest at the time — learn to spot my own patterns, stop suppressing emotions before they make me sick, actually share myself with the people I love, and figure out how to set goals that mean something to me rather than just chasing external validation.

Scored myself 1-3 out of 10 across the board. I had no idea that 14 weeks later I'd be sitting in the same chair (well, Zoom) scoring 6-8s and meaning it.

milestones.title: Session 2 — The AI Deep Dive System
milestones.summary: Told Meg about Claude and the framework I'd built for doing the work between sessions. She was intrigued.
milestones.story:
Somewhere between sessions 1 and 2, I'd started going deep. Really deep. I'd set up an AI system — Claude — to help me do structured reflections, deep dives, journaling prompts. Not as a replacement for therapy but as a way to do the actual work between sessions.

I told Meg about it and shared the Thais Gibson attachment theory coursework I'd been working through. She was genuinely curious — said she'd be keen to hear how I found it after another week or two.

Looking back, this was the moment the pattern of doing 90% of the work outside of therapy started. Not intentionally. I just couldn't help myself once I got started.

milestones.title: Session 3 — Malaysia Zoom
milestones.summary: First Zoom session — I was in Malaysia. Switched last minute. The work doesn't stop just because I moved.
milestones.story:
Was meant to be in-person but Laura and I were in Malaysia. Asked Peaceful Mind to switch to Zoom the day before. Meg set up a permanent link for us going forward — most sessions have been Zoom since.

The fact I didn't skip it matters. Old Pat would've used the trip as an excuse to 'take a break' from something that was getting uncomfortable. This Pat didn't even consider it.

milestones.title: Session 4 — Values Cards
milestones.summary: Meg brought out the values exercise. Started separating what I actually value from what I inherited.
milestones.story:
Meg sent me a set of values cards to print and cut out before the session. The exercise was about sorting through them — what actually matters to me versus what I'd absorbed from Mum's conditional approval system, from the business world, from trying to be what everyone else needed.

This was harder than it sounds. When you've spent your whole life performing a version of yourself that gets approval, untangling which values are genuinely yours takes real honesty.

milestones.title: Lesson 13: The Pattern
milestones.summary: Found the operating system. The codependency. The covert contracts. Saw the whole thing clearly for the first time.
milestones.story:
This was the big one. Working through Thais Gibson's material on codependency, I saw the complete picture of how I operate in relationships — especially with Laura.

I'd been giving 'genuinely'. Or so I thought. But underneath every generous act was an invisible contract: if I give enough, if I'm good enough, if I do enough — she'll love me back the same way. The contract was never spoken, never agreed to, and Laura had no idea it existed. When she didn't fulfill a contract she never signed, I'd feel hurt and confused. Classic covert contract.

Saw it clearly in therapy with Meg. Felt deep remorse. Not guilt — remorse. The difference matters. Guilt says 'I'm bad.' Remorse says 'I did something that doesn't match who I want to be, and I'm going to change it.'

The hardest areas to admit: household duties and paying for experiences. I knew I was over-giving there. I also knew I couldn't trust myself to set boundaries in the moment — I'd need predetermined ones.
milestones.quote: There is plenty of cash to stack later... you will be kicking yourself if you fast forward five years, $10M in the bank and you still feel empty and disconnected inside.

milestones.title: The Receiving Wound
milestones.summary: Someone gives to me and I freeze. Not because I don't want it — because a part of me doesn't believe I deserve it.
milestones.story:
Did a deep reflection on what happens when someone tries to give to me. First reaction: anxiety. A tightness. Like something's wrong and I need to figure out why they're being nice. Then — if I can sit with it — it transforms into this warm 'little kid' feeling. Like being five years old and someone just being kind to you for no reason.

The birthday tequila bottle tells the whole story. A mate gave me a really thoughtful bottle for my birthday. It's been sitting on my shelf for two years, untouched. I can't bring myself to open it. Not because I don't want it — because receiving something I didn't earn feels like borrowing something I'll have to return.

The wound isn't about willingness. It's about worthiness.

milestones.title: The Mother Wound
milestones.summary: Forgiven her. Still feel the longing. She was a scared girl doing her best with what she had.
milestones.story:
Did a visualisation exercise — imagined my young self not making school captain. Pictured Mum's face. The disappointment. The withdrawal of warmth. That was the blueprint: love is conditional on achievement. Stop achieving, stop being loved.

But here's what shifted — I've forgiven her. Genuinely. I can see her as a scared, insecure little girl whose own dad left when she was 13 or 14. She never processed it, never talked about it. Just passed the wound down.

Forgiving doesn't mean forgetting. I still feel the longing. That five-year-old part of me still wishes his mum loved him just because he existed, not because of what he could do. I've made peace with the fact that longing might always be there. It doesn't run me anymore, but it's there.

milestones.title: Boundaries = Safety
milestones.summary: Cracked the core belief: setting a boundary feels like causing an abandonment. Sat with 8/10 anxiety and brought myself down to 2/10. Alone.
milestones.story:
This was one of the most confronting nights. I was sitting with this massive anxiety — 8, maybe 9 out of 10 — and I couldn't figure out why. Dug into it and found the core belief sitting right there: Boundaries = rejection = abandonment = I'm not safe.

Every time I said yes to something I didn't want to do, it wasn't generosity. It was fear. Death by a thousand small yeses — saying yes to everything until I was completely depleted, resentful, and empty. Then blaming myself for feeling that way.

The biggest thing that happened that night: I regulated myself. No Meg, no Laura, no one. Just me, sitting with it, breathing through it, and watching the anxiety come down from 8-9 to 2-3. By myself.

That was the moment I realised the most important person who could give me love and safety that night was me. Not Laura, not Meg, not my mates. Me.
milestones.quote: The most important person who could give me love and safety tonight — was me.

milestones.title: Malaysia Evidence
milestones.summary: Set a real boundary with Laura on holiday. She was fine. I was free in 60 seconds. The fear was a ghost.
milestones.story:
We were in Malaysia and something came up where I needed to say no to something Laura wanted to do. Old Pat would've just said yes, swallowed the resentment, and added it to the pile.

This time I said what I actually felt. Clearly, kindly, but honestly.

Laura's response? Completely fine. Not upset, not distant, not punishing. Just... fine. The whole thing was over in 60 seconds and I felt freer than I had in months.

That's the thing about the blueprint — it's been predicting catastrophic abandonment for 30+ years, and the evidence keeps showing the opposite. Every boundary I set, the people who matter respond with respect, not rejection.
milestones.quote: I don't need external approval to feel safe. I can be my own source.

milestones.title: Session 5 — Laura's Growing Too
milestones.summary: Something beautiful happening. As I open up, Laura's meeting me there. Her own fearful-avoidant patterns softening.
milestones.story:
This session was about noticing something I hadn't expected. As I've been doing all this work — being more vulnerable, setting boundaries, sharing more honestly — Laura has been shifting too. Without me asking, without me pushing.

She's a fearful avoidant. Her pattern is to pull away when things get too close. But as I've become safer with myself, she's become braver with me. Sharing things she wouldn't have before. Being vulnerable in ways that are clearly new for her.

Nobody told her to do this. I didn't give her a Thais Gibson curriculum. She's responding to the change in me organically. That's the most beautiful evidence that this work is real — it's not just changing me, it's changing the space between us.

milestones.title: Session 6 — Integration
milestones.summary: Things are clicking faster. Patterns that used to blindside me for months now show up in hours.
milestones.story:
This session felt different from the earlier ones. Less digging, more integrating. The heavy excavation work of the first couple of months was settling into something more natural.

Pattern recognition is running almost automatically now. Something happens, I feel a reaction, and instead of months or years of unconscious repetition before the horrifying 'oh shit, I've been doing it again' moment — I'm catching it in hours. Sometimes minutes. Not perfectly, but the system is running.

Meg and I talked about what this means for session frequency. The foundation is laid. The tools are working. The question is becoming less about 'what do I need to fix' and more about 'how do I keep growing.'

milestones.title: Laura's Praise Landed Clean
milestones.summary: Sunday night. Laura praised my emotional work. I received it. No deflecting, no performing, no needing more. Just... received it.
milestones.story:
Laura said something really kind about the emotional work I've been doing. Sunday night, both of us in a good place.

Here's what didn't happen: I didn't deflect it with a joke. I didn't minimise it. I didn't immediately do something for her to 'earn' the praise. I didn't feel the desperate hunger for more of it.

I just... took it in. It was warm. It was enough. I didn't need to chase the next hit.

The irony is devastating and beautiful at the same time: when I was depleted and desperate for her validation, it could never land. Seconds at best, never enough, always needing more. Now that I don't need it? It lands perfectly. Low and slow and clean.

The receiving wound lives in the depleted version of me, not the current one.
milestones.quote: When I needed it most it couldn't land. Now I don't need it, it lands beautifully.

milestones.title: The Two Modes
milestones.summary: Named the two versions of me that show up in our relationship. Depleted Pat vs. Present Pat. Same man, completely different wiring.
milestones.story:
Realised there are two modes operating in how I receive love from Laura:

**Depleted Mode:** Out of spoons. Late night. Maybe drinking. The blueprint kicks in hard — five-year-old ball-cut Pat, approval seeking, servant, fawning husband. Desperately needs Laura's praise but it never, ever sticks. Seconds at best. Never enough. Always hungry for the next hit. This is the wounded child running the show.

**Good Place Mode:** Rested. Present. Doing my work. Don't need her praise, still want it. When it comes, it's low and slow and non-time-sensitive. Nice to have, not urgent. This is adult Pat, secure in himself.

The gap between these two is the map of the entire wound. The work is about making 'good place mode' the default — not by avoiding depletion forever, but by having enough self-awareness to know which version of me is showing up in any given moment.

milestones.title: The Dad Inventory
milestones.summary: Wrote 5 stories about what Dad gave me. Scored 0/5 for ever saying any of it out loud. This work was pure joy.
milestones.story:
Sat down one evening and wrote about Dad. Not about wounds — about gifts. Five specific memories of things he gave me that shaped who I am.

Dad is Geoff. Retired educator — loved by thousands of students. Gay, came out about 10 years ago, separated from Mum, partner is Peter. I am profoundly proud of him for that. The man loves flowers, plants, nature. Gentle, patient, kind.

The five stories poured out. And with them came this realisation that hit me in the chest: every quality I actually like about myself — patience, empathy, understanding, the ability to make people feel safe — came from him. Not from the achievement machine. Not from the hustle. From watching a quiet man tend his garden and love people without conditions.

Scored myself 0 out of 5 for ever telling him any of this. Zero. The man who gave me my best qualities has never once heard me name them. That's changing.

This work was the opposite of the mother wound stuff. It wasn't heavy or painful. It was joyful. Like discovering money in a jacket you forgot you owned — it was always there, I just never looked.
milestones.quote: Achievement praise doesn't hold value. Patient, understanding, empathy praise feels wonderful — and it's because of Dad I'm like that.

milestones.title: Mother Work vs. Dad Work
milestones.summary: Named the two types of inner work. Mum's gets me back to baseline. Dad's takes me above it.
milestones.story:
Working through the Dad Inventory surfaced a framework that makes the whole journey make sense:

**Mother work = back to baseline.** It's healing. Undoing the conditional love programming, the performance-based worth, the deleted emotions. Necessary, hard, sometimes painful. Gets me from negative to zero.

**Dad work = above baseline.** It's identity-building. Recognising what was planted in me, naming the qualities I actually value, shifting my self-worth source from achievements to character. Gets me from zero to flourishing.

The antidote to the mother wound isn't more processing of the mother wound. It's recognising what Dad planted — because that's where the real me lives.

The framework for the inventory: 'Don't write the speech. Write the source. The speech writes itself when the source is honest.' Did the source work. The phone call to actually say it shouldn't be scripted.
milestones.quote: Don't write the speech. Write the source. The speech writes itself when the source is honest.

milestones.title: The Anger Discovery
milestones.summary: Found the missing emotion. 4 months, 50+ pages of deep work, and anger didn't appear once. Turns out I'd deleted it entirely.
milestones.story:
Asked Garry to do a deep analysis of everything — every journal entry, every reflection, every deep dive, every assessment. 50+ pages across 4 months. The question: is there anything I'm avoiding, deflecting, or subconsciously skipping?

The answer came back clear: anger. Not once. Not a single expression of anger in 4 months of the most honest work I've ever done. Every other emotion showed up — sadness, fear, shame, love, grief, joy. But anger? Gone. Completely absent.

I kept the wiring of the smoke alarm and threw away the batteries.

Dug into why and found the origin: growing up watching Mum berate and belittle Dad. Anger was a weapon. It destroyed the person I loved most. So young Pat made a decision — anger is dangerous, delete it entirely, replace it with fawning.

The conversion mechanism is wild: something crosses a line, anger fires for a microsecond, then the system intercepts it and converts it to understanding or self-blame or problem-solving. So fast I don't even register that anger was the original signal.

So I did the thing I always do — fell down the well. Wrote about anger. Felt anger. For the first time, I expressed real, sustained, visceral anger about Mum — 'disgustingly mean,' 'fucking pathetic,' 'repulsive' — without apologising, without softening, without wrapping it in compassion to make it palatable.

And then — after the anger had space to breathe — compassion showed up too. Not as a replacement. Alongside. Both at once. That was new.

Then I caught something else: I'd been mocking self-love people for years. Cringe content, eye-rolling, the works. That wasn't cynicism — that was anger wearing a comedy mask. Safe targets, zero abandonment risk. I was mocking the exact medicine I needed.

Meg's cute response when I told her Garry found the gap: 'That's the kind of thing you've got me for, ask me!' Fair point.
milestones.quote: Anger isn't the opposite of peace. Suppressed anger is.

milestones.title: The Conversion Mechanism
milestones.summary: Mapped exactly how I delete anger before it reaches consciousness. It fires, gets intercepted, and turns into understanding.
milestones.story:
The mechanism works like this, and it's lightning fast:

1. Something crosses a line
2. Anger fires — the real emotion, the appropriate signal
3. Before it reaches conscious awareness, the system intercepts it
4. Converts it to understanding ('they're just stressed'), self-blame ('maybe I caused it'), or problem-solving ('how do I fix this')
5. The anger never registers. I genuinely believe I 'don't get angry'

What I actually did was make a point not to be destructive with anger — but I threw the baby out with the bathwater. The emotion itself got deleted, not just the destructive expression of it.

The breakthrough: realising that my distancing from Mum wasn't calm, mature understanding doing its job. It was anger doing its job — signalling 'this is repulsive, protect yourself.' I'd been crediting the wrong emotion for one of the best decisions I've made. Life got dramatically better after that boundary. Anger made the call. I just refused to acknowledge it.
milestones.quote: I kept the wiring of the smoke alarm and threw away the batteries.

milestones.title: Session 7 — The Check-In
milestones.summary: Reviewed original goals from Session 1. Scored 6-8 across the board. 90% of the work happened outside this room. Moving to monthly.
milestones.story:
This session was fun. Light. Felt like catching up with a really capable, secure friend who gets me — which is exactly what Meg has become.

She pulled up the goals I'd set in Session 1. I'd completely forgotten I'd even set goals. Went through them one by one and scored myself now versus then. The numbers were genuinely surprising — not because I was being generous, but because the evidence was undeniable.

The thing that made Meg smile the most: about 90% of the actual self-work has happened outside of therapy sessions. The deep dives, the journaling, the reflections, the anger discovery, the dad inventory — all done between sessions. She said that's the ideal way: 'Actual change happens in your life, not sitting in a doctor's office whingeing about your problems for years without putting in practice outside of that.'

This wasn't intentional. I didn't have a grand plan. I just trusted my instincts and got started, every single time, with no idea what I was doing. Every single time it was scary, and every single time it turned out to be wonderfully positive in ways I couldn't have imagined.

Moving to every 4 weeks. Not because the work is done — because the engine is running on its own now.

Also corrected Garry for suggesting my personal development should be prioritised above everything else. 'Calm down pussycat. I'm a peaceful motherfucker, but I'm still hungry.' Mapped out an around-the-world trip with Laura in 10 minutes — Tomorrowland in Belgium, near my birthday. There is not a bees dick chance in hell that the Pat I was up until 35 would have dared to dream this up.
milestones.quote: I'm a peaceful motherfucker, but I'm still hungry.

milestones.title: Kev's Gift
milestones.summary: The night that cracked me open. My mate Kev lovingly told me I wouldn't let anyone in. The vulnerable masculine love I needed.
milestones.story:
This wasn't recent — but I named it properly for the first time today during the goals check-in.

Kev sat me down one night and said something that changed everything. Not aggressively, not as a criticism. With love: 'You are such an amazing friend and supportive man for me and our friends, and you do it so well in such a kind, gentle, safe way. But you don't allow anyone to do even a little bit of that for you.'

That was the vulnerable, beautiful, masculine love I needed so much. Coming from Kev — one of my closest mates — it landed in a way that no therapist or textbook could have delivered. This was a man who loved me telling me I was locked behind glass.

That was the turning point for sharing. Not a therapy technique. Not a framework. A mate who loved me enough to say the uncomfortable thing.
milestones.quote: You are such an amazing friend and supportive man... but you don't allow anyone to do even a little bit of that for you.

highlights: Little Pat safety with adult Pat: 9/10
Receiving from Laura: 6/10 (lowest score on entire assessment)
Identified depleted vs. good-place receiving modes

keyPeople.laura: Wife. Fearful avoidant. Growing alongside Pat. Her vulnerability is expanding in real time.
keyPeople.meg: Therapist. Principal Clinical Psychologist at Peaceful Mind. Dynamic feels like a capable, secure friend who gets him.
keyPeople.dad: Geoff. Retired educator, loved by thousands. Gay, partner Peter. The gift Pat never named out loud. Joyful relationship.
keyPeople.kev: Inner circle. Delivered the roast that cracked Pat open. Pivotal moment in vulnerability.
keyPeople.boys: Ando, Kev, Tank, Julzy, Huggard. The inner circle. Relationships expanded massively since Pat started sharing.
